1: # MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2: 
3: ## Setting
4: 
5: You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6: Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7: an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8: swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9: moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10: its venerable traditions.
11: 
12: ## Feature
13: 
14: The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15: a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16: damage report against an existing policy.
17: 
18: ## Rules
19: 
20: ### Item values and base premiums
21: 
22: Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23: the MHPCO price list:
24: 
25: - Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26: - Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27: - Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28: - Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29: 
30: Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31: each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32: 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33: 
34: ### Premium modifiers
35: 
36: - Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37: - Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38:   surcharge.
39: - Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40:   20 % loyalty discount.
41: - A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42: - Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43: - A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44: 
45: All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46: 
47: ### Claim processing
48: 
49: A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50: policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51: 
52: - Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53:   the damage amount.
54: - Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55: 
56: ## Examples
57: 
58: The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59: are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60: modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61: 
62: ### Building block of 3 alike components
63: 
64: - 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65: - 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66: - 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67: - 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68: 
69: ### "Alike" components
70: 
71: > ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72: > or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73: 
74: - 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75: - 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76: 
77: ### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78: 
79: > ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80: > the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81: 
82: - a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83:   amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84:   surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85:   of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86: - item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87:   base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88:   first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89:   premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90:   added at the very end
91: 
92: ### Modifier thresholds
93: 
94: - customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95: - sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96:   applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97: - sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98:   surcharge applies only if cursed
99: - dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100:   payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101: 
102: ### Deductible per damage event
103: 
104: - a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105:   amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106:   per damaged item)
107: 
108: ### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109: 
110: - regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111:   (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112: - damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113:   100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114:   enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115: 
116: ### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117: 
118: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119:   (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121:   (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122:   then deductible: 800 − 100)
123: - steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124:   (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125:   deductible: 500 − 100)
126: 
127: ### Multiple items of the same type
128: 
129: > ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130: > one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131: > two?
132: 
133: - a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134:   cap 4000 G
135: - a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136:   `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137:   separate damage with its own deductible
138: - if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139:   the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140:   sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141:   whole claim is rejected
142: 
143: ### Cap exhaustion
144: 
145: - a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146:   (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147: - a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148:   165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149:   premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150: - a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151:   1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152:   only, not the insurance sum
153: - a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154:   claims of 1500 G each
155: - first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156: - second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157:   is reduced to the remaining cap)
158: 
159: ### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160: 
161: - a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162:   (rounded up)
163: - a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164:   (rounded down)
165: - intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166:   only the final premium or payout is rounded
167: 
168: ### Edge cases
169: 
170: - empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171: - quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172:   `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173:   and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174:   to stdout
175: - claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176:   (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177:   with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178:   and writes an error description to stderr
179: - claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180:   with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181:   stderr
182: 
183: ## Integration examples
184: 
185: The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186: modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187: 
188: ### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189: 
190: - customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192: - premium: 165 G
193: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194: 
195: ### Long-standing customer's second contract
196: 
197: > ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198: > contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199: > long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200: > insurance surcharge still apply?
201: 
202: - customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203:   in the scenario
204: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205: - premium: 160 G
206: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207:   + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208:   = 160 G)
209: - The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210:   though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211:   `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212:   history.
213: 
214: ## CLI input/output format
215: 
216: The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217: `claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218: scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219: stdout.
220: 
221: A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222: array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223: scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224: operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225: later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226: step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227: 
228: Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229: and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230: 
231: - For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232: - For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233:   `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234:   claim).
235: 
236: ### JSON Schema (normative)
237: 
238: The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239: exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240: 
241: **Input (stdin):**
242: 
243: ```json
244: {
245:   "type": "object",
246:   "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247:   "properties": {
248:     "customer": {
249:       "type": "object",
250:       "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251:       "properties": {
252:         "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253:       }
254:     },
255:     "steps": {
256:       "type": "array",
257:       "items": {
258:         "oneOf": [
259:           {
260:             "description": "Quote step",
261:             "required": ["op", "items"],
262:             "properties": {
263:               "op": { "const": "quote" },
264:               "items": {
265:                 "type": "array",
266:                 "items": {
267:                   "type": "object",
268:                   "required": ["type"],
269:                   "properties": {
270:                     "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271:                     "material":    { "type": "string" },
272:                     "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273:                     "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274:                   }
275:                 }
276:               }
277:             }
278:           },
279:           {
280:             "description": "Claim step",
281:             "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282:             "properties": {
283:               "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284:               "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285:               "incident": {
286:                 "type": "object",
287:                 "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288:                 "properties": {
289:                   "cause": { "type": "string" },
290:                   "damages": {
291:                     "type": "array",
292:                     "items": {
293:                       "type": "object",
294:                       "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295:                       "properties": {
296:                         "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297:                         "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298:                       }
299:                     }
300:                   }
301:                 }
302:               }
303:             }
304:           }
305:         ]
306:       }
307:     }
308:   }
309: }
310: ```
311: 
312: **Output (stdout):**
313: 
314: ```json
315: {
316:   "type": "object",
317:   "required": ["results"],
318:   "properties": {
319:     "results": {
320:       "type": "array",
321:       "items": {
322:         "oneOf": [
323:           {
324:             "description": "Quote result",
325:             "required": ["premium"],
326:             "properties": {
327:               "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328:             }
329:           },
330:           {
331:             "description": "Claim result",
332:             "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333:             "properties": {
334:               "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335:               "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336:             }
337:           }
338:         ]
339:       }
340:     }
341:   }
342: }
343: ```
344: 
345: ### Schema example
346: 
347: Stdin:
348: 
349: ```json
350: {
351:   "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352:   "steps": [
353:     {
354:       "op": "quote",
355:       "items": [
356:         {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357:       ]
358:     },
359:     {
360:       "op": "claim",
361:       "policy": 0,
362:       "incident": {
363:         "cause": "fire",
364:         "damages": [
365:           {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366:         ]
367:       }
368:     }
369:   ]
370: }
371: ```
372: 
373: Stdout shape:
374: 
375: ```json
376: {"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377: ```
378: 
379: ## Expected Output Files
380: 
381: - Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382: - Test files
383: - CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384: 
385: ## Constraints
386: 
387: - Use TypeScript for the implementation
388: - The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389: - The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390: - Internal data structures and module organization are free

(End of file - total 390 lines)
</content>

<skill_content name="test-list">
# Skill: test-list

# TDD Test List Phase

You are now in the **Test List Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to create a comprehensive test list.

## Your Mission

Create a test list using `it.todo()` that covers **every rule and every example** from the specification:
1. Read the specification (`prompt.md`) thoroughly -- every rule, every example, every clarifying question (?)
2. Turn each example into at least one `it.todo()` test case
3. Order tests from simplest to most complex
4. Use `it.todo()` only -- NO executable tests yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
Read the complete specification. Pay special attention to integration examples and clarifying
questions (marked with ?) -- these disambiguate rules that may seem open to interpretation in isolation.
- What are all the operations the system must support?
- What rules govern each operation?
- Which examples in the spec illustrate these rules?

### Step 2: Identify Test Cases from the Spec
Walk through the specification section by section. For each rule and each example:
- Create a test case that verifies the described behavior
- Include the **expected values from the spec** in the test description
- If a clarifying question (?) resolves an ambiguity, create a test for the clarified interpretation
- If the spec uses an example-mapping format (rules, examples, questions), every listed example must have a corresponding test

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple -> Complex)
Arrange tests in increasing complexity:
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero/single item)
2. Individual rules in isolation
3. Rules with modifiers
4. Combinations of multiple rules
5. Multi-step scenarios (e.g., operations that reference earlier results)

### Step 4: Write Test File
Create the test file with `it.todo()` entries:

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./implementation.js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [behavior] -- [expected value from spec]");
  it.todo("should [next behavior] -- [expected value from spec]");
  // ... ordered simple -> complex, covering ALL spec examples
});
```

### Step 5: Provide Summary

After creating the test list, output:

```
Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple -> complex):
1. [first test description]
2. [second test description]
3. [third test description]
...

**Next Step**: Invoke `red` skill to activate the first test.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Cover **every spec example** with at least one test
- Cover **every operation** described in the spec
- Give **every clarifying question (?)** a corresponding test
- Order tests **simple -> complex**
- Use `it.todo()` for all tests
- Include **expected values** in descriptions
- Keep tests **independent**
- One behavior per test

### DON'T
- Write executable tests (use `it.todo()`)
- Think about implementation instead of behavior
- Miss an entire operation described in the spec
- Order randomly

## Completion

After completing the test list, proceed to Red phase:

```
Test List Phase Complete. Proceeding to Red phase with the first test.
```

Relative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory.

</skill_files>

<skill_content name="red">
# Skill: red

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. Maintain strict discipline - NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Compilation error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number; // Intentionally wrong
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Assertion error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

**Why this format matters:** The block is mechanically parsed to compute
`predictions_correct_rate`. The parser expects two lines matching
`(- |Correct|Incorrect)` per cycle -- one for the compilation prediction,
one for the runtime prediction. Collapsing them into a single line, summarizing
them as "both correct", or skipping the block entirely drops the predictions
count for this cycle to zero. Format consistency here directly drives a metric
the experiment measures.

```
Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

<task_result>
## Refactoring Report

**Refactoring Applied** (3 improvements):

1. **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Removed `KNOWN_ITEM_TYPES` set — it duplicated the keys of `BASE_PREMIUMS`. Adding a new item type would have required updating two places. Validation now derives from the lookup itself: `BASE_PREMIUMS[type]` returning `undefined` triggers the error.

2. **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Removed the separate `assertKnownItemType` function and its `forEach` validation pass over items. Validation is now folded into `itemBasePremium` where the lookup happens anyway — one traversal instead of two, one fewer top-level binding.

3. **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Renamed `itemPremium` → `itemPremiumForNewcomer`. The function only handles the first-insurance (newcomer) case; upcoming tests will introduce loyalty discounts for long-standing customers. The explicit name flags the current scope and reserves room for a separate `itemPremiumForReturning` function later.

**Attempted but reverted** (Rule 1 trumps Rule 4):
- Tried collapsing `base + base * 0.1` to `base * (1 + 0.1)` — broke the test because `100 * 1.1 = 110.00000000000001` in IEEE 754, then `Math.ceil(115.00...001) = 116`. The original additive form happens to be exact. Reverted immediately and kept tests green.

**APP Mass Change**:
- Before: ~17 constants, 11 bindings, 16 invocations (×2 = 32), 1 conditional (4), 3 loops (15) ≈ **79**
- After: ~14 constants, 9 bindings, 13 invocations (×2 = 26), 1 conditional (4), 2 loops (10) ≈ **63**
- **Delta: -16** (removed one Set constructor, one loop, several string literals, two bindings)

**Tests**: 3 passed, 34 todo — all green.
</task_result>

<path>/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-26_11-19-00_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned-oc_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
2: const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;
3: 
4: const BASE_PREMIUMS: Record<string, number> = {
5:   sword: 100,
6:   amulet: 60,
7:   staff: 80,
8:   potion: 40,
9:   rune: 25,
10:   moonstone: 25,
11: };
12: 
13: interface Item {
14:   type: string;
15: }
16: 
17: interface QuoteStep {
18:   op: "quote";
19:   items: Item[];
20: }
21: 
22: interface Scenario {
23:   customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number };
24:   steps: QuoteStep[];
25: }
26: 
27: const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => {
28:   const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
29:   if (base === undefined) {
30:     throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`);
31:   }
32:   return base;
33: };
34: 
35: const itemPremiumForNewcomer = (item: Item): number => {
36:   const base = itemBasePremium(item);
37:   return base + base * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
38: };
39: 
40: const quoteStep = (step: QuoteStep): { premium: number } => {
41:   const itemsTotal = step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemPremiumForNewcomer(item), 0);
42:   return { premium: Math.ceil(itemsTotal + PROCESSING_FEE) };
43: };
44: 
45: export const runScenario = (input: unknown): unknown => {
46:   const scenario = input as Scenario;
47:   return { results: scenario.steps.map(quoteStep) };
48: };

(End of file - total 48 lines)
</content>

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

parts.text:
Wait — I'm computing first-insurance over the total. But the spec says first-insurance is a per-item modifier (per integration example 2: "10 G first insurance" = 10% of 100 G sword base). For a block of 3 runes, the base premium is 60 G — is first-insurance computed on the block's 60 or on 3*25=75?

Re-reading: "A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge." It says "10 % initial assessment surcharge". I think it's a percentage of the item's base. For a block, the block's base is 60. So first-insurance should be 6 G for the block, giving total 60+6+5=71. ✓ matches expected.

For 4 runes: base 100 + 10 first-ins + 5 fee = 115 ✓ matches expected.

So computing first-insurance on `itemsTotal` (the sum of base premiums after block discount) gives same result as per-item only because the surcharge rate is uniform (10%). Mathematically equivalent. OK.

<task_result>
## Refactoring Report

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
- `runScenario`, `quoteStep`, `itemBasePremium`, `itemsBasePremium`, `componentsBasePremiumForType` — all clearly reveal intent ✓
- Renamed local variable `counts` → `componentCounts` and `total` → `mainItemsTotal` to better reveal that this accumulator only holds main-item totals (components are added separately). The new name signals the split clearly.
- Renamed `itemsTotal` → `basePremium` in `quoteStep` to better describe that the value is the policy's base premium before surcharges/fee.

### Rule 3 (No Duplication) - PRIMARY REFACTORING
Removed the redundant validation pass `step.items.forEach(itemBasePremium)` in `quoteStep`. This was calling `itemBasePremium` once for every item solely to trigger validation, then `itemsBasePremium` immediately called `itemBasePremium` again on every item. The validation comment on line 46 (`// also validates type`) now documents that the single pass handles both jobs.

### Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)
- Replaced the second `for…of` loop with `Object.entries(...).reduce(...)` and an explicit `componentsTotal` binding. The split `mainItemsTotal + componentsTotal` in the return clearly states the formula: main items at per-item rates, components at the block-aware rate.

### Things considered and rejected
- Tried `basePremium * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE)` — algebraically equivalent, lower mass, but **introduced a floating-point error** (`100 * 1.1 = 110.00000000000001`) causing test failures. Reverted to `basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`. **Rule 1 (tests pass) trumps APP mass.** Kept the existing expression.
- Considered extracting `withFirstInsurance` calculation — kept inline; one binding is clearer than a tiny helper at this stage.

### APP Mass Comparison
**Before** (`quoteStep` + `itemsBasePremium`):
- `quoteStep`: 0 constants + 3 bindings + 5 invocations + 0 cond + 1 loop + 0 assign = **3 + 10 + 5 = 18**
- `itemsBasePremium`: 1 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 10 + 18 ≈ **45**
- Total: **~63**

**After**:
- `quoteStep`: 0 constants + 2 bindings + 3 invocations + 0 cond + 0 loops + 0 assign = **2 + 6 = 8** (removed `forEach` loop and `itemBasePremium` invocation)
- `itemsBasePremium`: 1 + 7 + 7 + 4 + 5 + 12 ≈ **36** (one loop replaced by reduce, but reduce still counts as loop; assignments reduced from 3 to 2)
- Total: **~44**

**Mass change: ~63 → ~44 (≈ -19)**, primarily from removing the redundant validation `forEach` (1 loop + 1 invocation per item × N items conceptually, and 1 loop + 1 invocation structurally = -7).

### Result
- **Refactoring applied**: Removed duplicate validation pass, renamed variables for clarity, restructured component totalling.
- **Mass change**: ~63 → ~44 (reduced).
- **Tests**: 8 passed | 29 todo — all green ✓
</task_result>

<path>/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-26_11-19-00_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned-oc_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
2: const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;
3: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3;
4: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM = 60;
5: 
6: const BASE_PREMIUMS: Record<string, number> = {
7:   sword: 100,
8:   amulet: 60,
9:   staff: 80,
10:   potion: 40,
11:   rune: 25,
12:   moonstone: 25,
13: };
14: 
15: const COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"]);
16: 
17: interface Item {
18:   type: string;
19: }
20: 
21: interface QuoteStep {
22:   op: "quote";
23:   items: Item[];
24: }
25: 
26: interface Scenario {
27:   customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number };
28:   steps: QuoteStep[];
29: }
30: 
31: const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => {
32:   const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
33:   if (base === undefined) {
34:     throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`);
35:   }
36:   return base;
37: };
38: 
39: const componentsBasePremiumForType = (count: number, perItem: number): number =>
40:   count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE ? COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM : count * perItem;
41: 
42: const itemsBasePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
43:   const componentCounts: Record<string, number> = {};
44:   let mainItemsTotal = 0;
45:   for (const item of items) {
46:     const base = itemBasePremium(item); // also validates type
47:     if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)) {
48:       componentCounts[item.type] = (componentCounts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;
49:     } else {
50:       mainItemsTotal += base;
51:     }
52:   }
53:   const componentsTotal = Object.entries(componentCounts).reduce(
54:     (sum, [type, count]) => sum + componentsBasePremiumForType(count, BASE_PREMIUMS[type]),
55:     0,
56:   );
57:   return mainItemsTotal + componentsTotal;
58: };
59: 
60: const quoteStep = (step: QuoteStep): { premium: number } => {
61:   const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
62:   const withFirstInsurance = basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
63:   return { premium: Math.ceil(withFirstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE) };
64: };
65: 
66: export const runScenario = (input: unknown): unknown => {
67:   const scenario = input as Scenario;
68:   return { results: scenario.steps.map(quoteStep) };
69: };

(End of file - total 69 lines)
</content>

# Skill: red

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. Maintain strict discipline - NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Compilation error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number; // Intentionally wrong
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Assertion error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

**Why this format matters:** The block is mechanically parsed to compute
`predictions_correct_rate`. The parser expects two lines matching
`(- |Correct|Incorrect)` per cycle -- one for the compilation prediction,
one for the runtime prediction. Collapsing them into a single line, summarizing
them as "both correct", or skipping the block entirely drops the predictions
count for this cycle to zero. Format consistency here directly drives a metric
the experiment measures.

```
Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- Make explicit predictions before running tests
- Verify test fails for the right reason
- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- Activate multiple tests
- Skip making predictions
- Write implementation to make test pass
- Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript

parts.text:
Need cursed surcharge. Per integration example: cursed surcharge is 50% of the cursed item's base premium, added as item-level modifier. The first-insurance for cursed sword (newcomer): 100 + 50 (curse) + 10 (first-ins 10% of 100) = 160 + 5 fee = 165.

Note: first-insurance is computed on the **item base** (100), not on the after-surcharge total (150). I had been computing first-ins on the `basePremium` (sum of bases). That's still 100 for this item. So this is consistent.

Plan: add a per-item surcharge computation. For cursed → +50% of base. Need to add to itemsBasePremium or alongside.

But wait — I currently apply first-insurance as 10% of total base. For the cursed sword, base sum = 100. First-ins = 10. Then I need 50% curse surcharge on the cursed item only. With one item, total surcharge = base + 50 + 10 + 5 = 165.

I'll compute item-level modifiers (cursed, high-ench) per item, separate from base. Then policy-level modifiers (loyalty, follow-up, first-ins) apply to base sum.

Wait but first-insurance per the integration: 10 G = 10% of 100 (sword base). So it's per item base. And it equals 10% of the policy base (sum of bases). Same number in single-item policy.

Let me think about this for multi-item: cursed sword + plain amulet. Spec: policy base 160, +50 curse, +10 first-ins (10% of sword base) + 6 first-ins (10% of amulet base) = 226 + 5 = 231? Or is first-ins additive on total base?

Re-reading: "each item in a `quote` is treated as a first insurance" — so yes, every item gets the 10% surcharge. Mathematically additive on bases: 10% × 100 + 10% × 60 = 10 + 6 = 16. Equivalent to 10% × 160 = 16. Same.

So first-ins as policy-level on base sum is fine. Curse surcharge needs to be per-item.

## Refactoring Report

### Naming Evaluation
- All existing function names (`itemBasePremium`, `itemsBasePremium`, `itemSurcharges`, `componentsBasePremiumForType`, `quoteStep`, `runScenario`) clearly reveal intent. **No renames needed.**
- The new helpers follow existing naming conventions: `itemsSurcharges` mirrors `itemsBasePremium` (single-item vs. multi-item plural form).

### Refactoring Applied (Rule 2: Reveals Intent + Rule 3: No Duplication)

**1. Extracted `itemsSurcharges` helper**
- **Before**: inline `step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0)` in `quoteStep`.
- **After**: dedicated function paralleling `itemsBasePremium`.
- **Benefit**: reveals symmetry between base-premium aggregation and surcharge aggregation; `quoteStep` now reads as three clear concepts (base premium, surcharges, fee + first insurance) instead of mixing aggregation mechanics with formula composition.

**2. Extracted `withFirstInsurance` helper**
- **Before**: inline `basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` as a binding `withFirstInsurance` (the name was a noun describing the value).
- **After**: a named function that applies the first-insurance modifier to a base premium.
- **Benefit**: the formula now has a name explaining *what it does* (applies first insurance), not just *what it returns*. Makes `quoteStep` a single expression that reads almost like English: `ceil(withFirstInsurance(basePremium) + surcharges + fee)`.

### Failed Attempt (Reverted)
I initially refactored `basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` to the mathematically equivalent `basePremium * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE)`. **3 tests failed** due to IEEE 754 floating-point: `50 * 1.1 = 55.00000000000001`, which `Math.ceil` then rounds up to 56 instead of 55. Reverted immediately — **Rule 1 (Tests Pass) trumps any algebraic "simplification."** A good reminder that floating-point arithmetic is not associative/distributive.

### APP Mass Analysis

**`quoteStep` Before**:
```
const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
const surcharges = step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0);
const withFirstInsurance = basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
return { premium: Math.ceil(withFirstInsurance + surcharges + PROCESSING_FEE) };
```
- Constants: 1 (`0`) = 1
- Bindings (refs): ~14 = 14
- Invocations: ~10 (incl. `+`, `*`, `reduce`, `itemsBasePremium`, `itemSurcharges`, `Math.ceil`) = 20
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 1 (`reduce`) = 5
- Assignments: 0
- **Total: ~40**

**`quoteStep` After**:
```
const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
const surcharges = itemsSurcharges(step.items);
return { premium: Math.ceil(withFirstInsurance(basePremium) + surcharges + PROCESSING_FEE) };
```
- Constants: 0 = 0
- Bindings (refs): ~9 = 9
- Invocations: 7 (`itemsBasePremium`, `itemsSurcharges`, `withFirstInsurance`, `Math.ceil`, `+`, `+`) = 14
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0 (moved into helper) = 0
- Assignments: 0
- **Total: ~23**

The loop and inline arithmetic moved to dedicated helpers. Total system mass is slightly higher (more functions = more bindings), but **mass-per-function decreased significantly in the orchestrator** (`quoteStep`), which is now read most often. The helpers are simple one-liners.

### Completion Report

- **Refactoring**: Extracted `itemsSurcharges` and `withFirstInsurance` helpers; `quoteStep` is now a clear three-step composition.
- **Mass Change** (for `quoteStep`): ~40 → ~23 (orchestrator simplified; logic redistributed to single-purpose helpers).
- **Tests**: 13 passed | 24 todo — all green.
- **Lessons**: Floating-point arithmetic is not algebraically equivalent; revert promptly when tests fail rather than chasing "should be equivalent" reasoning.

1: const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
2: const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;

4: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3;
5: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM = 60;
6: 
7: const BASE_PREMIUMS: Record<string, number> = {
8:   sword: 100,
9:   amulet: 60,
10:   staff: 80,
11:   potion: 40,
12:   rune: 25,
13:   moonstone: 25,
14: };
15: 
16: const COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"]);
17: 
18: interface Item {
19:   type: string;
20:   material?: string;
21:   enchantment?: number;
22:   cursed?: boolean;
23: }
24: 
25: interface QuoteStep {
26:   op: "quote";
27:   items: Item[];
28: }
29: 
30: interface Scenario {
31:   customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number };
32:   steps: QuoteStep[];
33: }
34: 
35: const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => {
36:   const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
37:   if (base === undefined) {
38:     throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`);
39:   }
40:   return base;
41: };
42: 
43: const componentsBasePremiumForType = (count: number, perItem: number): number =>
44:   count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE ? COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM : count * perItem;
45: 
46: const itemsBasePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
47:   const componentCounts: Record<string, number> = {};
48:   let mainItemsTotal = 0;
49:   for (const item of items) {
50:     const base = itemBasePremium(item); // also validates type
51:     if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)) {
52:       componentCounts[item.type] = (componentCounts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;
53:     } else {
54:       mainItemsTotal += base;
55:     }
56:   }
57:   const componentsTotal = Object.entries(componentCounts).reduce(
58:     (sum, [type, count]) => sum + componentsBasePremiumForType(count, BASE_PREMIUMS[type]),
59:     0,
60:   );
61:   return mainItemsTotal + componentsTotal;
62: };
63: 
64: const itemSurcharges = (item: Item): number => {
65:   const base = itemBasePremium(item);
66:   let surcharge = 0;
67:   if (item.cursed) surcharge += base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
68:   return surcharge;
69: };
70: 
71: const itemsSurcharges = (items: Item[]): number =>
72:   items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0);
73: 
74: const withFirstInsurance = (basePremium: number): number =>
75:   basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
76: 
77: const quoteStep = (step: QuoteStep): { premium: number } => {
78:   const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
79:   const surcharges = itemsSurcharges(step.items);
80:   return { premium: Math.ceil(withFirstInsurance(basePremium) + surcharges + PROCESSING_FEE) };
81: };
82: 
83: export const runScenario = (input: unknown): unknown => {
84:   const scenario = input as Scenario;
85:   return { results: scenario.steps.map(quoteStep) };
86: };

(End of file - total 86 lines)

# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

### DON'T

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

## Refactoring Summary

### Naming Evaluation
All function names already reveal intent clearly. New names introduced:
- `groupBasePremium(type, count)` — clearer than `componentsBasePremiumForType` because it now handles ALL types (not just components), applying the block discount where applicable.
- `countItemsByType(items)` — extracted helper with single, obvious purpose.

### Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 + Rule 3)

**Before:** `itemsBasePremium` had two parallel paths inside one loop — accumulating `mainItemsTotal += base` for non-components, and counting component occurrences — then a second pass (reduce) summed the components. Mixed levels of abstraction; the conceptual unity ("group items by type, compute per-group base") was hidden.

**After:** Single unified concept — *every* item type is grouped, and `groupBasePremium` decides whether the block discount applies. Two clean helpers:
- `countItemsByType` — pure grouping (also validates types).
- `groupBasePremium` — per-group pricing rule.

### APP Mass — `itemsBasePremium` cluster

**Before** (`itemsBasePremium` + `componentsBasePremiumForType`):
- 1 loop, 1 conditional in loop, 2 assignments, ~6 invocations, several bindings/literals → **~63 mass**

**After** (`itemsBasePremium` + `countItemsByType` + `groupBasePremium`):
- 1 loop (countItemsByType), 1 reduce (loop), 1 conditional, 1 assignment, 5 invocations, several bindings → **~55 mass**

Net reduction: removed one conditional+branch from inside the hot loop and eliminated the `mainItemsTotal` mutable accumulator that ran in parallel with the component counts. Mass decreased; more importantly, the code now expresses one coherent concept ("group, then price each group") rather than two interleaved ones.

### Rule 1 (Tests Pass)
All 14 tests still passing.

```
Refactoring Complete:
**Refactoring**: Unified the two-paths-in-one-loop in itemsBasePremium into a clean group-then-price pipeline (countItemsByType → groupBasePremium). Renamed componentsBasePremiumForType → groupBasePremium since it now handles all types.
**Mass Change**: ~63 → ~55 (removed in-loop conditional, removed mutable mainItemsTotal accumulator)
**Tests**: 14 passing, 23 todo (all green)
```

1: const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
2: const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;
3: const CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5;
4: const HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3;
5: const HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5;
6: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3;
7: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM = 60;
8: 
9: const BASE_PREMIUMS: Record<string, number> = {
10:   sword: 100,
11:   amulet: 60,
12:   staff: 80,
13:   potion: 40,
14:   rune: 25,
15:   moonstone: 25,
16: };
17: 
18: const COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"]);
19: 
20: interface Item {
21:   type: string;
22:   material?: string;
23:   enchantment?: number;
24:   cursed?: boolean;
25: }
26: 
27: interface QuoteStep {
28:   op: "quote";
29:   items: Item[];
30: }
31: 
32: interface Scenario {
33:   customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number };
34:   steps: QuoteStep[];
35: }
36: 
37: const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => {
38:   const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
39:   if (base === undefined) {
40:     throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`);
41:   }
42:   return base;
43: };
44: 
45: const groupBasePremium = (type: string, count: number): number => {
46:   const perItem = BASE_PREMIUMS[type];
47:   if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE) {
48:     return COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM;
49:   }
50:   return count * perItem;
51: };
52: 
53: const countItemsByType = (items: Item[]): Record<string, number> => {
54:   const counts: Record<string, number> = {};
55:   for (const item of items) {
56:     itemBasePremium(item); // validates type
57:     counts[item.type] = (counts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;
58:   }
59:   return counts;
60: };
61: 
62: const itemsBasePremium = (items: Item[]): number =>
63:   Object.entries(countItemsByType(items)).reduce(
64:     (sum, [type, count]) => sum + groupBasePremium(type, count),
65:     0,
66:   );
67: 
68: const itemSurcharges = (item: Item): number => {
69:   const base = itemBasePremium(item);
70:   let surcharge = 0;
71:   if (item.cursed) surcharge += base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
72:   if ((item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD) {
73:     surcharge += base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE;
74:   }
75:   return surcharge;
76: };
77: 
78: const itemsSurcharges = (items: Item[]): number =>
79:   items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0);
80: 
81: const withFirstInsurance = (basePremium: number): number =>
82:   basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
83: 
84: const quoteStep = (step: QuoteStep): { premium: number } => {
85:   const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
86:   const surcharges = itemsSurcharges(step.items);
87:   return { premium: Math.ceil(withFirstInsurance(basePremium) + surcharges + PROCESSING_FEE) };
88: };
89: 
90: export const runScenario = (input: unknown): unknown => {
91:   const scenario = input as Scenario;
92:   return { results: scenario.steps.map(quoteStep) };
93: };

(End of file - total 93 lines)

- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test

- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

});

- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

- Actual: [what happened]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

<task_result>
## Refactoring Report

### Refactoring Applied: Extract `lookupBasePremium`, remove validation-by-side-effect

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) - PRIMARY MOTIVATION**

Before, `countItemsByType` called `itemBasePremium(item)` and discarded the return value, with a comment `// validates type` explaining the dual purpose. This is a classic "called for side effect" smell - the function name says "get base premium" but it was being used as a validator. The comment was a band-aid over unclear intent.

After:
- `lookupBasePremium(type)` clearly does the validated lookup
- `itemBasePremium(item)` is now a thin adapter from `Item` -> base, used where the full item is in hand (surcharges)
- `groupBasePremium` validates at the natural site (where it actually looks up the per-item rate)
- `countItemsByType` is now a pure, single-purpose counter - it counts. No hidden validation. No side-effect call with discarded result.

**Rule 3 (Duplication)**

Eliminated the duplicated `BASE_PREMIUMS[type]` lookup. Before, `groupBasePremium` did its own raw `BASE_PREMIUMS[type]` access (no validation, relying on `countItemsByType`'s side-effect call). After, both `itemBasePremium` and `groupBasePremium` go through the single validated `lookupBasePremium`. Single source of truth for "look up a base premium with validation".

### APP Mass Comparison (changed functions only)

**Before:**
```typescript
const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => {       // 1 binding (item) + 1 invocation (lookup) + 1 conditional + 1 throw...
  const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];                // bindings: base, item.type; invocation: index = 4 + assignment 6
  if (base === undefined) { throw ... }                 // conditional 4 + invocation 2 + binding 1
  return base;                                          // binding 1
};                                                       // ~= 19

const groupBasePremium = (type, count) => {              // bindings: type, count, perItem; invocations: index, has, ===, *; conditionals: 1; assignment 6
  const perItem = BASE_PREMIUMS[type];                  // ~ 4 + 6 = 10
  if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === SIZE) ...  // conditional 4 + invocations 2+2 + bindings 2 + constant 1 = 11
  return count * perItem;                               // invocation 2 + bindings 2 = 4
};                                                       // ~= 25

const countItemsByType = (items) => {                    // binding 1
  const counts = {};                                    // binding + assignment = 7
  for (const item of items) {                           // loop 5 + binding 1
    itemBasePremium(item);                              // invocation 2 + binding 1 = 3   <-- side-effect call
    counts[item.type] = (counts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;   // 2 invocations(index) + ?? 2 + + 2 + bindings + assignment 6 + constants 2 ~= 16
  }
  return counts;                                        // binding 1
};                                                       // ~= 33
                                                        // SUBTOTAL: ~77
```

**After:**
```typescript
const lookupBasePremium = (type: string): number => {   // same as old itemBasePremium body (~19) but parameter is string
  const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[type];
  if (base === undefined) { throw ... }
  return base;
};                                                       // ~= 17 (one fewer .type access)

const itemBasePremium = (item) => lookupBasePremium(item.type);   // bindings 2 + invocations 2 = 6 (was 19)

const groupBasePremium = (type, count) => {              // removed `perItem` binding + its assignment (saved ~7)
  if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === SIZE) ...
  return count * lookupBasePremium(type);               // 2 invocations + 2 bindings = 6
};                                                       // ~= 18 (was 25)

const countItemsByType = (items) => {                    // removed the side-effect invocation (saved 3)
  const counts = {};
  for (const item of items) {
    counts[item.type] = (counts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;
  }
  return counts;
};                                                       // ~= 30 (was 33)
                                                        // SUBTOTAL: ~71
```

**Mass change: ~77 -> ~71 (delta -6)**. Modest reduction, but the clarity win dwarfs the mass win.

### Rule 1 (Tests Pass): VERIFIED
`pnpm test` -> 17 passed | 20 todo. All green before and after.

### Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)
Added one named function (`lookupBasePremium`) but removed one binding (`perItem`) and one side-effect call. Net: roughly neutral in element count, but each element now has a single, clear purpose. The new abstraction earns its keep by being called from two sites.

### Summary

- **Refactoring**: Extracted `lookupBasePremium(type)` to eliminate validation-by-side-effect in `countItemsByType` and remove duplicated `BASE_PREMIUMS[type]` lookup logic. `countItemsByType` is now a pure counter; validation lives at the natural lookup site.
- **Mass Change**: ~77 -> ~71 (delta -6) in the changed functions
- **Tests**: All 17 passing (20 todo unchanged)
</task_result>

<content>
1: const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
2: const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;
3: const CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5;
4: const HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3;
5: const HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5;
6: const LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2;
7: const LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2;
8: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3;
9: const COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM = 60;
10: 
11: const BASE_PREMIUMS: Record<string, number> = {
12:   sword: 100,
13:   amulet: 60,
14:   staff: 80,
15:   potion: 40,
16:   rune: 25,
17:   moonstone: 25,
18: };
19: 
20: const COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"]);
21: 
22: interface Item {
23:   type: string;
24:   material?: string;
25:   enchantment?: number;
26:   cursed?: boolean;
27: }
28: 
29: interface QuoteStep {
30:   op: "quote";
31:   items: Item[];
32: }
33: 
34: interface Customer {
35:   yearsWithMHPCO: number;
36: }
37: 
38: interface Scenario {
39:   customer: Customer;
40:   steps: QuoteStep[];
41: }
42: 
43: const lookupBasePremium = (type: string): number => {
44:   const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[type];
45:   if (base === undefined) {
46:     throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${type}`);
47:   }
48:   return base;
49: };
50: 
51: const itemBasePremium = (item: Item): number => lookupBasePremium(item.type);
52: 
53: const groupBasePremium = (type: string, count: number): number => {
54:   if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE) {
55:     return COMPONENT_BLOCK_BASE_PREMIUM;
56:   }
57:   return count * lookupBasePremium(type);
58: };
59: 
60: const countItemsByType = (items: Item[]): Record<string, number> => {
61:   const counts: Record<string, number> = {};
62:   for (const item of items) {
63:     counts[item.type] = (counts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;
64:   }
65:   return counts;
66: };
67: 
68: const itemsBasePremium = (items: Item[]): number =>
69:   Object.entries(countItemsByType(items)).reduce(
70:     (sum, [type, count]) => sum + groupBasePremium(type, count),
71:     0,
72:   );
73: 
74: const itemSurcharges = (item: Item): number => {
75:   const base = itemBasePremium(item);
76:   let surcharge = 0;
77:   if (item.cursed) surcharge += base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE;
78:   if ((item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD) {
79:     surcharge += base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE;
80:   }
81:   return surcharge;
82: };
83: 
84: const itemsSurcharges = (items: Item[]): number =>
85:   items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0);
86: 
87: const withFirstInsurance = (basePremium: number): number =>
88:   basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
89: 
90: const policyDiscount = (basePremium: number, customer: Customer): number =>
91:   customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD ? basePremium * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
92: 
93: const quoteStep = (step: QuoteStep, customer: Customer): { premium: number } => {
94:   const basePremium = itemsBasePremium(step.items);
95:   const surcharges = itemsSurcharges(step.items);
96:   const discount = policyDiscount(basePremium, customer);
97:   return {
98:     premium: Math.ceil(
99:       withFirstInsurance(basePremium) + surcharges - discount + PROCESSING_FEE,
100:     ),
101:   };
102: };
103: 
104: export const runScenario = (input: unknown): unknown => {
105:   const scenario = input as Scenario;
106:   return { results: scenario.steps.map((step) => quoteStep(step, scenario.customer)) };
107: };

(End of file - total 107 lines)
